content:
We have now completed our study of Numbers 7:9 and the Kohathites what they carried, how they carried it, and what each holy object was pointing to in Christ and in our own calling as new creation believers. That has been a long and rich journey. Today we move forward in Numbers 7 to a passage that, at first reading, can seem like a repetition. In fact it is one of the most deliberately repetitive passages in all of Scripture, and that repetition is itself the message. From verse 10 to verse 88, the chapter records the dedication offerings brought by the twelve tribal leaders of Israel on twelve consecutive days following the completion and anointing of the Tabernacle and its altar.

Before we look at what was offered, we must understand when it was offered and why. These offerings were not part of the regular annual calendar of Israel. They were a one-time event that marked the completion of a specific season. The Tabernacle had been built. Moses had set it up. Every holy vessel inside had been anointed with oil and sanctified. Aaron and his sons had been consecrated and set apart for the priesthood. Everything was now in order. Everything was ready. And in response to the completion of this great work, the leaders of the twelve tribes came forward, one by one, over twelve days, to bring their offerings for the dedication of the altar.

Numbers 7:84 names what this season was: “This was the dedication of the altar, in the day when it was anointed, by the princes of Israel.” The word dedication speaks of a setting apart, a consecration, a formal presentation of something to God for His exclusive use. The altar was not being used for the first time in these days. It had already been anointed. But now the tribal leaders were bringing their gifts to honour what had been built and to present themselves and their tribes before Yahweh through these offerings. This was Israel’s collective act of worship at the moment the Tabernacle became fully operational.

What makes this passage stand out is the structure. Each of the twelve tribal leaders brought exactly the same offering. There was no variation in the items. There was no competition to see who could bring more. There was no leader who brought a larger gift to make his tribe look greater before Yahweh.

Day one and day twelve brought the same list. The first tribe and the last tribe stood equally before the altar. The only difference from one day to the next was the name of the leader and the tribe he represented. Everything else was identical.

Each leader brought silver vessels a silver plate weighing 130 shekels and a silver bowl weighing 70 shekels, both filled with fine flour mixed with oil for a grain offering. He brought one golden spoon or pan of 10 shekels, filled with incense. He brought a burnt offering of one young bull, one ram, and one male lamb of the first year. He brought a sin offering of one male goat. And he brought a peace offering of two oxen, five rams, five male goats, and five male lambs of the first year. This same list appears twelve times in the chapter once for each tribe word for word, item for item, weight for weight.

For the reader who comes to this chapter expecting variety, the repetition can feel heavy. But this is not careless writing. The repetition is the point. Every tribe stood before Yahweh with the same offering because every tribe stood before Yahweh with the same need. The great and the small tribes were equal before the altar. The tribe of Judah, which led the camp, brought no more than the tribe of Benjamin, which was the smallest. Before the face of God, every leader came with exactly what every other leader came with. The altar received all twelve the same.

At the close of the twelve days, Numbers 7:84-88 gives the full summary of everything that was offered. Across twelve days, twelve silver plates and twelve silver bowls were brought, with a total silver weight of 2,400 shekels. Twelve golden spoons filled with incense were brought, with a total gold weight of 120 shekels. For the burnt offerings: twelve young bulls, twelve rams, and twelve male lambs of the first year, each with their grain offerings. For the sin offerings: twelve male goats. For the peace offerings: twenty-four oxen, sixty rams, sixty male goats, and sixty male lambs of the first year. The scale of what was offered over these twelve days was enormous. And yet every single item came from the same list, repeated twelve times, by twelve different men, one day at a time.

One detail must not be missed in the counting. The silver of each plate was 130 shekels and each bowl was 70 shekels which together make 200 shekels per leader. Twelve leaders multiplied by 200 shekels each gives 2,400 shekels of silver in total. And the gold: each spoon was 10 shekels. Twelve spoons make 120 shekels of gold. Nothing about these numbers is accidental. In Scripture, silver consistently speaks of redemption the 30 pieces of silver, the half-shekel of atonement money, the price of a slave. And gold speaks of the divine character of what is offered. The silver and the gold together were filled with fine flour and incense the grain offering and the fragrance of worship. Even in the weight of the vessels, the offering was preaching.

After all twelve days, after all twelve leaders, after all the bulls and rams and lambs and goats and silver and gold had been offered at the altar of dedication, Numbers 7:89 records one final verse that stands apart from everything that came before it. “And when Moses went into the tabernacle of the congregation to speak with Yahweh, then he heard the voice of one speaking unto him from off the mercy seat that was upon the ark of testimony, from between the two cherubims: and He spake unto him.”

The offerings were finished. The twelve days were complete. The altar had been dedicated. And then Moses went in and heard the voice of Yahweh speaking to him from between the cherubim above the mercy seat. The dedication of the altar led directly to the voice of God being heard. The worship came first.

The offerings came first. The twelve days of faithful, repeated, unhurried presenting before Yahweh came first. And then in the silence after the last offering God spoke. This is not a coincidence. This is a pattern. The ear that hears the voice of God is prepared by the heart that has first brought its offering. We will study tomorrow what each part of these offerings points to spiritually and what it means for us today.

title: Prayer
text: Yahweh, You are the God who receives the offering of each tribe and each person equally, who does not honour the great above the small, who weighs the gift by its faithfulness and not by its size. We thank You for the twelve days of dedication offerings for the picture of twelve leaders standing before the same altar with the same gift, showing us that before Your face every one of Your people stands on the same ground. We thank You most of all for the verse that ends the chapter quietly that after the offerings were complete, Moses heard Your voice from between the cherubim. Teach us this order. Teach us to bring our offering first faithfully, consistently, day after day and then to wait in the silence for the voice. Help us not to rush past the altar looking for the voice while the offering is still in our hands. In Christ’s name, amen.
